7. Galois Theory

Recall an -homomorphism preserves roots in of a polynomial in . It can be viewed as a ring homomorphism. Since a field has only and itself as ideals, nontrivial homomorphisms are injective.

Let be a field extension of . Let be a -automorphism. Then it’s easy to check that permutes the roots of polynomials in . For example, the -automorphisms of are identity and conjugation.

7.3. Splitting Fields

Definition 47. Let be irreducible. Then is a “splitting field for over “ if

  1. splits completely in
  2. where are the roots of in .

Lemma 38 (Artin 16.3.1).

  1. Suppose . If is a splitting field of over , then is also a splitting field of over .
  2. Every has a splitting field.
  3. A splitting field is a finite extension of .
  4. A finite extension of is contained in a splitting field.
Proof
  1. Obvious
  2. Take extension field where splits completely (exists by Proposition 44). Take the subfield generated by the roots of .
  3. Since each is algebraic by constructino, is finite over .
  4. Let be a finite extension, and let be the splitting field of over where is the product of the irreducible polynomials of each .

□

Theorem 35 (Splitting Theorem). Let be a splitting field for . If is an irreducible polynomial with one root in , then splits completely in .
Proof

[TODO]

□

Thus we can redefine splitting fields as the having the following property, without having to specify a polynomial:

Definition 48. is a splitting field if any irreducible polynomial over with one root in splits completely in .

. The roots of the irreducible polynomial are . By Lemma 39, there exists a unique automorphism for mapping to another root. In particular we have two automorphisms, (identity) and (conjugation).

A similar result holds for quadratic number fields where .

Proof

7.5. Fixed Fields

Definition 50. The be a group of automorphisms on a field . The fixed field of , denoted , is the subset of elements in fixed by every element of .

(54)

Proposition 49. is a subfield of .

Theorem 36 (Artin 16.5.2). Let be a finite group of automorphisms of . Let , and let be the -orbit of .

  1. The irreducible polynomial over is
  2. Hence is algebraic over and has degree , which divides by orbit-stabilizer.
Proof
  1. Let . Each coefficient is a symmetric functions of the -orbit of and hence belongs to . Suppose is a root of . Then divides by considering the automorphisms. Hence generates the PID of polynomials with root (Artin 15.2.3).
  2. Follows immediately from (1).

□

Definition 51. is algebraic if every element of is algebraic over .
Lemma 40. If is algebraic and not a finite field extension, then there exists elements in whose degrees over are arbitrarily large.
Proof

Create a chain of strictly increasing fields: by choosing element . By hypothesis we can continue indefinitely.

□

Theorem 37 (Fixed Field Theorem). Let be a fixed group of automorphisms of and be its fixed field. Then is a finite extension of ; in particular .
Proof

Let . From Theorem 36, is an algebraic extension, and the degree of any divides . Let be a primitive element, guaranteed to be algebraic. By uniqueness (Lemma 39), the stabilizer must be the identity map so the orbit has order . By Theorem 36 has order and thus has degree .

□

7.6. Galois Extensions

Definition 52. is an intermediate field if . By definition, we have .

  1. The Galois group of a finite field extension is a finite group whose order divides .
  2. Let be a finite group of automorphisms of . Then is a Galois extension of , and .
Proof

  2. Immediate corollary of the Fixed Field Theorem, using the fact that is a subgroup of .

□

Lemma 42 (Artin 16.6.3). Let for primitive . Let be the irreducibile polynomial of , and let be the roots of in . There is a unique -automorphism of such that . These are all of the -atuomorphisms of , so has order .
Proof

Follows immediately from Lemma 39.

□

The notion of Galois extension and splitting field are the same, as we show below.

Theorem 39 (Characteristic Properties of Galois Extensions). Let be a finite extension and let be its Galois group. TFAE:

  1. is a Galois extension, i.e. .
  2. The fixed field is equal to .
  3. is a splitting field over .
Proof
  • (1) (2) is easy.
  • (1) (3) See iPad notes.

□

Cor 16.6.5.

  1. Every finite extension is contained in a Galois extension.
  2. If is a Galois extension and , then is a Galois extension and .
Proof

In both, we can replace notion of Galois extension with splitting field. We have proved both equivalent statements in Lemma 38.

□

Theorem 40 (Artin 16.6.6). Let be a Galois extension with Galois group . Let split completely in with roots .

  1. acts on the set of (unique) roots .
  2. If is a splitting field, the operation is faithful and embeds as a subgroup of .
  3. If is irreducible, the opertaion is transitive.
  4. If is a splitting field of over and is irreducible over , embeds as a transitive subgroup of .
Proof
  1. F-automorphisms permute roots.
  2. Recall faithful means that all nonidentity elemetns act nontrivially Follows from (1) of Lemma 39.
  3. Follows from Theorem 36.
  4. Follows from (2) and (3).

□

7.7. The Main Theorem

Theorem 41 (Main Theorem). Let be a Galois extension of a field , and let be its Galois group. There is a bijective correspondence between subgroups of and intermediate groups .

Forward direction: Send to its fixed field .
Reverse direction: Send to its Galois group .

Proof

It suffices to show that composing the two maps in either order is the identity map. By the Lemma 41 (corollary of Fixed Field Theorem), , proving the forward direction. The backward direction is proved by the corollary of Theorem 39.

The two sets have the same size if and only if the extension is Galois by definition.

□

Cor The correspondence given by the Main Theorem reverses inclusions: if and only if . where

Cor A finite extension has finitely many intermediate fields.

Proof

We can embed any finite extension into a splitting field (Galois extension), and then invoke the main theorem (a finite group has finitely many subgroups).

□

From Cor 16.6.5, we know that if given a chain of fields and is a Galois extension of , then is also a Galois extension of . But is not necessarily a Galois extension of . It turns out that normal subgroups correspond to Galois extensions of :

Theorem 42 (Artin 16.7.5). Let be a Galois extension with Galois group , and let be the fixed field of a subgroup of . The extension is a Galois extension if and only if is a normal subgroup of . If so, then the Galois group is isomorphic to the quotient group .
Proof

Read Artin/iPad.

□

  • Splitting field of over has Galois group .
  • has Galois group (see roots of unity section)
  • has Galois group ().

Let’s see how to apply Galois theory.

7.8. Cubic Equations

Let be an irreducible cubic polynomial, and be a splitting field (Galois extension) of over . By Vieta’s theorem, the sum of the roots is in so the third root is generated by the first two:

(55)

We have since is irreducible. We have equals 1 or 2 depending whether the polynomial is irreducible.

The Galois group is a transitive subgroup of and thus it is either or . We want to check whether is irreducible in . We’d rather work in the field . The next theorem allows this.

Let . Note that , , and swapping two roots mulitlpies by .

Theorem 43 (Galois Theory for Cubics). If is a square in (i.e. ), then . Otherwise .
Proof

means that it is fixed by every element of (-automorphism), meaning that cannot have any odd permutations. Otherwise, contains an odd permutation since only is fixed by .

□

quantifies how hard it is to solve by radicals. In particular, if , we only need one cube root, but if , we also need a square root.

When , is the unique intermediate field of degree . It’s unique by the main theorem.
